figures.caption: Fig. 1: Motivation of the proposed ATSS framework. Real videos are characterized by stochastic spatiotemporal dynamics, resulting in diffuse and low-magnitude self-similarity patterns. In contrast, AI-generated videos exhibit systemic temporal regularity caused by their anchor-driven generation, which yields denser and higher-intensity correlation matrices across multiple modalities. This anomalous self-similarity serves as a distinctive forensic fingerprint, enabling ATSS to effectively distinguish AIGVs from natural sequences.

TABLE I: Comparison of detection AP (%) between ATSS and 13 state-of-the-art baselines on GenVideo. † Results are reproduced using the officially released code. †† Results are reproduced from our implementation based on the original paper, as no official code is available.

TABLE II: Comparison of detection AUC (%) between ATSS and 13 state-of-the-art baselines on GenVideo. † Results are reproduced using the officially released code. †† Results are reproduced from our implementation based on the original paper, as no official code is available.

TABLE III: Comparison of detection AP (%) between ATSS and 13 state-of-the-art baselines on EvalCrafter. † Results are reproduced using the officially released code. †† Results are reproduced from our implementation based on the original paper, as no official code is available.

TABLE IV: Comparison of detection AUC (%) between ATSS and 13 state-of-the-art baselines on EvalCrafter. † Results are reproduced using the officially released code. †† Results are reproduced from our implementation based on the original paper, as no official code is available.
TABLE X: Ablation performance of individual components in ATSS on GenVideo, reported in terms of AP (%).
TABLE XII: Ablation performance of individual components in ATSS on GenVideo, reported in terms of ACC (%).
TABLE XI: Ablation performance of individual components in ATSS on GenVideo, reported in terms of AUC (%).

Fig. 2: The overall framework of ATSS. Given a video with T sampled frames, an image captioning model is first employed to generate frame-wise textual descriptions. Visual and textual features are then extracted to construct visual, textual, and crossmodal self-similarity matrices. Each matrix is processed by a dedicated Transformer to capture temporal dynamics. Finally, a cross-attentive fusion module integrates these modality-specific cues into a unified representation for binary classification.

Fig. 4: Visualization of Attention Density Maps. Each row displays the attention weights for the visual, textual, and cross-modal branches of a real video and ten AI-generated samples, which are randomly selected from MSR-VTT and each generator subset of GenVideo, respectively.

Fig. 3: t-SNE visualizations of 10 subsets on the GenVideo dataset: (a) Crafter, (b) Gen2, (c) HotShot, (d) Lavie, (e) ModelScope, (f) MoonValley, (g) MorphStudio, (h) Show-1, (i) Sora, and (j) WildScrape.

TABLE VI: Comparison of detection AUC (%) between ATSS and 13 state-of-the-art baselines on VideoPhy. † Results are reproduced using the officially released code. †† Results are reproduced from our implementation based on the original paper, as no official code is available.

TABLE V: Comparison of detection AP (%) between ATSS and 13 state-of-the-art baselines on VideoPhy. † Results are reproduced using the officially released code. †† Results are reproduced from our implementation based on the original paper, as no official code is available.
TABLE XIII: Ablation results of ATSS using various image captioning backbones and visual-textual encoders on the GenVideo dataset, measured by AP (%).
TABLE XV: Ablation results of ATSS using various image captioning backbones and visual-textual encoders on the GenVideo dataset, measured by ACC (%).
TABLE XIV: Ablation results of ATSS using various image captioning backbones and visual-textual encoders on the GenVideo dataset, measured by AUC (%).
